eli3: The dot product measures how aligned two vectors are. Multiply corresponding components and sum: [1,2] · [3,4] = 1×3 + 2×4 = 11. If vectors point the same direction, the dot product is large; if perpendicular, it's zero.

Dot product = ||a|| ||b|| cos(θ). This means it encodes both the lengths of the vectors and the angle between them. Unit vectors (length 1) make the dot product equal to cos(θ) — a pure measure of alignment.

Projection finds the 'shadow' of one vector onto another. If you shine a light perpendicular to vector b, the projection of a onto b is how much of a points in b's direction. Formula: proj_b(a) = ((a·b)/(b·b)) b.

whenWhy.title: Measuring similarity in NLP
whenWhy.body: Cosine similarity (dot product of normalized vectors) measures semantic similarity between word embeddings or document vectors. High cosine = similar meaning. Used in search, recommendations, and clustering.

whenWhy.title: Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization
whenWhy.body: Projections recursively remove components along previous basis vectors to create orthogonal sets. QR decomposition uses this to factor A = QR where Q is orthogonal and R is upper triangular.

whenWhy.title: Least squares and optimization
whenWhy.body: The normal equations XᵀXβ = Xᵀy come from orthogonality: residuals are orthogonal to column space of X. Project y onto span(X) to find the closest point in the column space.

whenWhy.title: Physics and mechanics
whenWhy.body: Work = force · displacement (dot product). Only the force component in the direction of motion does work. Similarly, projections separate vector quantities into parallel and perpendicular components.

deepDiveIntro: The dot product of two vectors is computed by multiplying corresponding components and summing the results. Geometrically, it equals the product of the two vectors' lengths and the cosine of the angle between them — so it encodes both magnitude and alignment in a single number.

The projection of vector a onto vector b finds how much of a points in b's direction. It is a scalar multiple of b in the direction of b. The leftover part — a minus its projection onto b — is perpendicular to b. Repeatedly subtracting projections is how the Gram-Schmidt process builds a set of orthogonal vectors.

deepDive.heading: Dot Product Properties
deepDive.body: The dot product turns two vectors into a single number that reflects both their size and their alignment. It behaves linearly and does not depend on the order of the vectors, which makes it easy to manipulate algebraically. When a vector is dotted with itself, the result is its squared Euclidean length.

deepDive.heading: Orthogonality
deepDive.body: Two vectors are orthogonal when they have no component pointing along each other, which is the geometric idea of being perpendicular. Collections of orthonormal vectors are especially valuable because they give clean coordinates and preserve distances. Matrices built from orthonormal columns represent rigid motions such as rotations and reflections.
deepDive.formula: a ⟂ b ⟺ a·b = 0,   Q orthogonal ⟺ QᵀQ = I

deepDive.heading: Vector Projection
deepDive.body: Projecting one vector onto another isolates the part that points along the chosen direction. The result is the closest vector on that line to the original vector, and the leftover difference is perpendicular to the line. This is the geometric idea behind least squares and many matrix factorizations.

deepDive.heading: Gram-Schmidt Process
deepDive.body: The Gram-Schmidt process turns a linearly independent set of vectors into an orthogonal or orthonormal basis. It works by repeatedly removing the parts already explained by earlier directions and then rescaling what remains. More stable variants are preferred in numerical software because repeated subtraction can amplify round-off error.
deepDive.formula: uₖ = aₖ − Σⱼ₌₁ᵏ⁻¹ proj_qⱼ(aₖ),  qₖ = uₖ/||uₖ||

deepDive.heading: Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality
deepDive.body: The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality says that the alignment between two vectors can never exceed the product of their lengths. This is why cosine similarity always stays between negative one and one. The result appears everywhere in geometry, probability, and optimization because it gives a universal upper bound on inner products.
deepDive.formula: |a·b| ≤ ||a|| ||b||,  equality iff a = cb for some scalar c

takeaways: Dot product a·b = Σ aᵢbᵢ = ||a|| ||b|| cos(θ) measures alignment.
Orthogonal vectors have a·b = 0. Orthonormal sets form bases with nice properties.
Projection of a onto b: proj_b(a) = ((a·b)/(b·b)) b.
Gram-Schmidt orthogonalizes vector sets using successive projections.
Cauchy-Schwarz: |a·b| ≤ ||a|| ||b||, with equality for parallel vectors.

edgeCases.q: What is the geometric interpretation of negative dot product?
edgeCases.a: Negative dot product means the angle between vectors is obtuse (> 90°). They point in generally opposite directions. In ML, this means embeddings represent opposite or antonymous concepts.

edgeCases.q: Why is modified Gram-Schmidt better than classical?
edgeCases.a: Classical Gram-Schmidt computes projections using the original vectors, accumulating round-off errors. Modified version subtracts projections sequentially using already-orthogonalized vectors, maintaining better numerical orthogonality.

Projection onto the zero vector is undefined (division by zero). In practice, this means you can't project onto a zero vector, and numerical issues arise when projecting onto very small vectors due to loss of precision.

sources.label: Strang — Introduction to Linear Algebra (6th ed.), Ch. 1
